context: customer focus as a strategy

my_sentence: Keep focus on your product.
correction: Keep focusing on your product.
explanation: The pattern "keep" + verb uses the -ing form: "keep focusing on". "Keep focus on" is not idiomatic; you could also say "stay focused on".
correct_examples: Keep focusing on your goals. | Stay focused on the task.
context: brand and product strategy

my_sentence: If you try to make a new product, it will failed in the end.
correction: If you try to make a new product, it will fail in the end.
explanation: (assistant note) After "will" use the base form "fail". "Fail" is intransitive, so don't make it passive ("be failed"): "it will fail in the end".
correct_examples: The plan will fail without support. | The project failed in the end.
context: launching new products

my_sentence: When your company become bigger and bigger, you need to recruit many employees.
correction: When your company becomes bigger and bigger, you need to recruit many employees.
explanation: The third-person singular subject "your company" takes "becomes" in the present simple.
correct_examples: When the company grows, it hires more people. | As it becomes bigger, costs rise.
context: providing a good environment for employees

my_sentence: One of my coworkers did his job pretty good.
correction: One of my coworkers did his job pretty well.
explanation: Use the adverb "well" to describe how he did his job, not the adjective "good".
correct_examples: He did his job well. | She speaks English well.
context: a story about a coworker who lost his job

my_sentence: It is not only one person can finish it.
correction: It is not only one person who can finish it.
explanation: Introduce the relative clause that modifies "person" with "who": "one person who can finish it".
correct_examples: She's the only one who can fix it. | He's not the only person who knows.
context: building a brand image takes many people

my_sentence: No bad things happen.
correction: Nothing bad happened.
explanation: You were reporting about a finished day, so use the past simple 'happened'. Also prefer the single word 'nothing' over 'no bad things' for a more natural negative.
correct_examples: Nothing bad happened today. | Nothing went wrong during the upgrade.
context: Teacher asked why Jack looked gloomy

my_sentence: No one want to apply it.
correction: No one will apply for it.
explanation: You 'apply for' a job/position — the preposition 'for' is required before the object. This recurred today (you also said 'I will apply it'). Note 'no one' takes a singular verb.
correct_examples: She applied for the job last week. | I'll apply for it tomorrow.
context: Talking about government jobs in the countryside vs. city

my_sentence: If a position is in the city center, many people will apply; however, if it in the countryside, no one will apply.
correction: If a position is in the city center, many people will apply for it; if it is in the countryside, no one will apply.
explanation: Every clause needs a verb — 'if it in the countryside' is missing 'is'. Keep the parallel 'if ..., (then) ...' structure for both cases instead of inserting 'however' between them.
correct_examples: If it is in the city, people apply; if it is far away, no one does. | If the job is well paid, applicants come; if it isn't, they don't.
context: Comparing city-center and countryside government positions

my_sentence: If a company can't change their strategies quickly, and he will go bankrupt.
correction: If a company can't change its strategies quickly, it will go bankrupt.
explanation: A company is referred to with 'it/its', not 'he'. Also don't put 'and' before the main clause of an if-sentence — the if-clause already links the two.
correct_examples: If a company can't adapt, it will fail. | The firm changed its strategy, so it survived.
context: Why companies cut their workforce

my_sentence: After Covid-19, most company run out of their money.
correction: After Covid-19, most companies ran out of money.
explanation: 'most' + a countable noun takes the plural ('companies'), and a finished past event needs the past tense 'ran'.
correct_examples: Most companies cut costs that year. | Many firms ran out of cash during the pandemic.
context: Why workforce cutbacks became common

my_sentence: So the situation become more serious.
correction: So the situation became more serious.
explanation: You were describing a past development, so use the past simple 'became'.
correct_examples: The situation became serious after the layoffs. | Things became harder once sales dropped.
context: Effect of Covid-19 on companies

my_sentence: We regarded economy is important by that time.
correction: We regarded the economy as important at that time.
explanation: The verb 'regard' uses the pattern 'regard something AS something', not 'regard ... is'. Add the article 'the economy', and use 'at that time' for a point in the past. (assistant note)
correct_examples: We regarded the economy as our top priority. | They regarded him as a good leader.
context: When China's economy recovered

my_sentence: Being a doctor, is it respected by others?
correction: If you're a doctor, most people will respect you.
explanation: The passive 'is it respected by others' sounds awkward; it's more natural to make people the subject of an active sentence.
correct_examples: If you're a teacher, students respect you. | People respect doctors in my country.
context: Whether doctors are respected

my_sentence: If you don't effort, you can't catch them.
correction: If you don't make an effort, you can't seize them.
explanation: 'effort' is a noun, not a verb — say 'make an effort'. And you 'seize/grab' an opportunity rather than 'catch' it. (assistant note)
correct_examples: If you don't make an effort, you'll fall behind. | You have to seize every opportunity.
context: What to do to avoid losing your job

my_sentence: If he or she can responsibility, that will be pretty good.
correction: If he or she can be responsible, that's pretty good.
explanation: After the modal 'can', use 'be + adjective (responsible)', not the noun 'responsibility'. ('be' + adjective describes the person.)
correct_examples: A good worker can be responsible. | She can be very reliable when needed.
context: What makes someone a good worker

my_sentence: Volunteer is not popular in my country.
correction: Volunteering is not popular in my country.
explanation: When the subject is the activity itself, use the gerund 'Volunteering', not the bare verb/noun 'volunteer'.
correct_examples: Volunteering is common among students. | Recycling is encouraged here.
context: Whether volunteering is popular

my_sentence: I have never do anything for community service.
correction: I have never done anything for community service.
explanation: The present perfect needs the past participle 'done' after 'have never', not the base form 'do'.
correct_examples: I have never done that before. | She has never worked abroad.
context: Whether Jack has done community service

my_sentence: Most people know me.
correction: Most of them know me.
explanation: You'd just mentioned the people in your office, so replace the repeated noun with a pronoun ('them') to avoid repetition. The teacher reminded you of this twice today.
correct_examples: My colleagues are friendly; most of them know me. | I've met the team, and most of them remember me.
context: Whether everyone at work knows Jack

my_sentence: Even you do the same thing, you can earn more in the sales department.
correction: Even if you do the same thing, you can earn more in the sales department.
explanation: To introduce a hypothetical, use 'even if' (or 'even though' for a real fact) — not bare 'even'. This recurred several times today.
correct_examples: Even if you work hard, choices matter more. | Even though it's cheap, it sells well.
context: Why the sales department pays more

my_sentence: Most companies that sell product to customers depend on marketing.
correction: Most companies that sell products to customers depend on marketing.
explanation: Countable nouns used in a general sense take the plural — 'products' (and likewise 'coupons'). The teacher corrected this several times today.
correct_examples: They sell products online. | The store hands out coupons.
context: Which companies rely on marketing

my_sentence: They want to recruit some idols or peoples.
correction: They want to recruit some idols or people.
explanation: 'people' is already the plural of 'person', so never add -s to make 'peoples' (except 'peoples' = different ethnic groups/nations).
correct_examples: They recruit talented people. | Many people applied for the role.
context: Comparing the entertainment industry with networking

my_sentence: We don't have a share department.
correction: We don't have a sales department.
explanation: 'sales' (/seɪlz/) kept coming out as 'share'. The team that sells is the 'sales department' — keep the s/l sound distinct from 'sh'.
correct_examples: She works in the sales department. | Our sales team met the client.
context: Whether Jack's company has a sales department

my_sentence: The products can be sure on the shelf.
correction: The products can be shown on the shelf.
explanation: 'shown' (the past participle of 'show', /ʃoʊn/) was coming out as 'sure'/'shoe'. Use 'can be + past participle': 'can be shown'.
correct_examples: These items can be shown on the top shelf. | The data can be shown in a chart.
context: Who decides which products are displayed in stores

my_sentence: Filipino Peso is the smallest unit.
correction: The Philippine peso is the smallest currency; the smallest currency unit is the cent.
explanation: Money is a 'currency', not a 'unit'. The smallest subdivision of a currency is the 'cent' (e.g. 100 cents = 1 peso).
correct_examples: The yen is Japan's currency. | One hundred cents make a dollar.
context: Comparing currencies of China, Japan and the Philippines

my_sentence: You can pay one point nine nine.
correction: You can pay nine ninety-nine. (9.99)
explanation: When reading an amount, don't say the cents digit by digit. Read the decimal part as a whole number: 9.99 = 'nine ninety-nine', 19.99 = 'nineteen ninety-nine'. (Reading digit by digit is for codes, plate numbers and addresses, not amounts.)
correct_examples: It costs nineteen ninety-nine. | The total is twelve fifty.
context: Talking about prices ending in .99

my_sentence: I had some free time to learn new knowledge.
correction: I had some free time to learn some new vocabulary words.
explanation: In English you 'learn vocabulary words' (or just 'new words'); 'learn knowledge' is not a natural collocation — you 'gain' or 'acquire' knowledge.
correct_examples: I spent the evening learning new vocabulary words. | She acquired a lot of knowledge from the course.
context: How Jack spent his day

my_sentence: What of the other?
correction: What about the others?
explanation: To ask for the next point, use the fixed phrase 'What about ...?', not 'What of ...?'. Since you mean the rest of the people, use the plural pronoun 'the others'.
correct_examples: That's one option. What about the others? | What about the other team members?
context: Discussing people who praise vs. people who complain

my_sentence: When we find some problems, it bothers me, it bothers also.
correction: When we find problems, it will bother us.
explanation: You were speaking generally about everyone, so the object should be 'us', not 'me'. Use 'will bother' for the expected result.
correct_examples: When the system fails, it will bother us all. | These bugs bother us every day.
context: Why people complain about problems

my_sentence: They will complain our system.
correction: They will complain about our system.
explanation: 'complain' needs the preposition 'about' before the thing you're unhappy with: 'complain about something'.
correct_examples: Customers complain about the long wait. | He always complains about the weather.
context: Customers complaining about the store system

my_sentence: Cost, quality and deliver time.
correction: Cost, quality and delivery time.
explanation: Before a noun, use the noun form 'delivery' ('delivery time'), not the verb 'deliver'.
correct_examples: We compare price, quality and delivery time. | Fast delivery time is a selling point.
context: Different perspectives for judging a product

my_sentence: If you make a mistake, you need to apology for it.
correction: If you make a mistake, you need to apologize for it.
explanation: 'apology' is the noun; the verb is 'apologize'. After 'need to' you need the verb: 'apologize for it'.
correct_examples: You should apologize for being late. | He apologized for the mistake.
context: What to do after making a mistake at work

my_sentence: They don't want to talk with you.
correction: They don't want to talk to you.
explanation: Here the natural preposition with 'talk' is 'to' — 'talk to someone'. ('talk with' exists, but the teacher preferred 'talk to you'.)
correct_examples: I need to talk to you for a minute. | Nobody wanted to talk to him after that.
context: How a bad attitude affects coworkers

my_sentence: You need to ask other to join.
correction: You need to ask others to join you.
explanation: Use the plural 'others' for other people, and add the object 'you' — they join you.
correct_examples: You can ask others to join you on the project. | She asked the others to help her.
context: You can't finish a project alone

my_sentence: They will preach / prize / pray about it.
correction: They will praise it.
explanation: 'praise' (/preɪz/) kept coming out as 'pray', 'prize' or 'preach'. Keep the long /eɪ/ followed by a /z/ sound, distinct from those other words.
correct_examples: The manager will praise good work. | She praised the team for their effort.
context: People who find things to praise

my_sentence: I go there one time a year, or one time in two years.
correction: I go there once a year, or once in every two years.
explanation: For frequency, use 'once' (one time) and 'twice' (two times) instead of 'one time' / 'two times'. For every second year, say 'once in every two years'.
correct_examples: I see the dentist twice a year. | We meet once in every two years.
context: How often Jack visits the dental clinic

my_sentence: Taiwan only has 24 millions people.
correction: Taiwan only has about 25 million people.
explanation: After an exact number, 'million' stays singular: '25 million people'. Only use 'millions of' (with -s) when there is no specific number.
correct_examples: The city has three million residents. | Millions of people watched the game.
context: Comparing the populations of China and Taiwan

my_sentence: I don't have pretty good communicating skills.
correction: I don't have pretty good communication skills.
explanation: The fixed compound noun is 'communication skills' — singular 'communication' plus plural 'skills'. Not 'communicating' or 'communications'.
correct_examples: Good communication skills are essential. | The job requires strong communication skills.
context: Talking about Jack's communication ability

my_sentence: For me, I don't have pretty good communication skills.
correction: My communication skills aren't pretty good.
explanation: It's more natural to make 'my communication skills' the subject than to say 'I don't have good ...'. Also drop the redundant 'For me, I'.
correct_examples: My writing skills aren't very strong yet. | His English isn't very good.
context: Assessing his own communication skills

my_sentence: I can't understand others mining.
correction: I can't understand what others mean.
explanation: Use the noun clause 'what others mean' as the object of 'understand'. ('mean' is the verb here; 'mining' was a slip.)
correct_examples: I can't understand what they mean. | Do you understand what I mean?
context: Difficulty in communication

my_sentence: I need to prepare many times.
correction: I need to prepare for it many times.
explanation: When there's an object, 'prepare' takes 'for': 'prepare for it/something'.
correct_examples: I need to prepare for the meeting. | They prepared for the exam all week.
context: Why Jack can't give a speech without a script

my_sentence: The result is not certified.
correction: The result is not satisfying.
explanation: To say a result isn't good enough, use 'satisfying' (or 'satisfactory'). 'certified' means officially approved — a different meaning.
correct_examples: The outcome wasn't satisfying. | Her answer was satisfactory.
context: Why an unprepared speech turns out badly

my_sentence: We more care about the result.
correction: We care more about the result.
explanation: The adverb 'more' comes after the verb 'care': 'care more about ...', not 'more care about'. The teacher corrected this several times today.
correct_examples: We care more about quality than speed. | I care more about the team than the title.
context: What modern tech companies value

my_sentence: Like assembly line worker, security guard and nanny.
correction: Like assembly-line workers, security guards and nannies.
explanation: When talking about a type of job in general, use the plural: 'workers', 'security guards', 'nannies'.
correct_examples: Jobs like nurses and teachers help people. | Security guards work long shifts.
context: Jobs where 'arrive early, work late' applies

my_sentence: If they always late and forget something, you will let them go.
correction: If they are always late and forget things, you will let them go.
explanation: 'late' is an adjective, so it needs the verb 'be': 'they are always late'. Don't drop the 'are'.
correct_examples: If he is always late, he'll be fired. | They are always on time.
context: Why nannies should not arrive late

my_sentence: They need to dress in a business-like way to show their professional and respect.
correction: They need to dress in a business-like way to show their professionalism and respect.
explanation: 'professional' is an adjective; here, after 'their' and joined with the noun 'respect', you need the noun 'professionalism'.
correct_examples: A suit shows your professionalism. | They valued his professionalism and honesty.
context: Why doctors and business people dress formally

my_sentence: I will learn new skills to improve my effective.
correction: I will learn new skills to improve my effectiveness. / ...to make my projects more effective.
explanation: 'effective' is an adjective, so it can't follow 'my'. Use the noun 'effectiveness' ('my effectiveness'), or keep the adjective before a noun ('effective projects'). Forms: effect (noun), effective (adjective), effectively (adverb).
correct_examples: Training improved my effectiveness at work. | The new tool makes the team more effective.
context: How learning skills makes you valuable

my_sentence: So we need to make ourselves valuable. So it is very important to improve our skills.
correction: We need to make ourselves valuable, so it is very important to improve our skills.
explanation: 'so' is a conjunction that joins clauses, so avoid starting a sentence with it. Either join the two clauses with 'so', or begin the new sentence with a different word. The teacher flagged this repeatedly today.
correct_examples: The deadline is close, so we must hurry. | It was raining, so we stayed inside.
context: Making yourself valuable so you won't be fired

my_sentence: First it can help the client. Second it can improve professionalism.
correction: First, it can help the client. Secondly, it can improve professionalism.
explanation: When listing points, use the ordinal adverbs 'First(ly), Secondly, Thirdly' (followed by a comma), not the bare adjective 'Second'.
correct_examples: Firstly, it saves money. Secondly, it saves time. | Secondly, we should test the code.
context: Listing the advantages of dressing formally

my_sentence: If others don't know about it, you can't get promotion.
correction: If others don't know about it, you can't get promoted.
explanation: Say 'get promoted' (passive verb) or 'get a promotion' (with the article 'a'). 'get promotion' without an article isn't natural.
correct_examples: He worked hard and got promoted. | She's hoping to get a promotion this year.
context: Why visibility matters for keeping your job

my_sentence: I felt fine and did.
correction: I feel fine today.
explanation: The teacher asked "How are you today?", which calls for a present-tense answer about your current state. Use the simple present ("I feel fine"), not the past tense.
correct_examples: I feel fine today. | How are you? I'm doing well, thanks.
context: Greeting at the start of class

my_sentence: I eat some vegetables and meat for my dinner.
correction: I ate vegetables and meat for dinner.
explanation: Dinner already happened, so use the past tense "ate", not the present "eat". Also drop "my" before "dinner" — "for dinner" is the natural phrasing.
correct_examples: I ate vegetables and meat for dinner. | What did you have for lunch?
context: Talking about what he ate

my_sentence: When they meet some difficult things, they can insist.
correction: When they meet difficulties, they can insist.
explanation: Use the noun "difficulties" instead of the wordy "difficult things". English has a dedicated noun form, which sounds more natural and concise.
correct_examples: When they face difficulties, they keep going. | She overcame many difficulties.
context: Writing about staying positive

my_sentence: They can insist taking them on time and time again.
correction: They can insist on taking them on time and time again.
explanation: "Insist" takes the preposition "on" before a gerund: "insist on doing something". Don't drop the "on".
correct_examples: He insisted on paying the bill. | She insists on doing it herself.
context: Writing about staying positive

my_sentence: Others will be negative.
correction: Others will feel negatively.
explanation: To describe how people react emotionally, the teacher preferred "feel negatively" (verb + adverb) over "be negative" here. Use the adverb "negatively" to modify the verb "feel".
correct_examples: Some people react negatively to criticism. | They felt negatively about the change.
context: Contrasting positive and negative reactions

my_sentence: If something make me feel bored, it is negative for me.
correction: If something makes me feel bored, it is negative for me.
explanation: "Something" is third-person singular, so the verb needs an -s: "something makes", not "something make".
correct_examples: If something makes me happy, I do it. | Everything depends on the weather.
context: Defining positive vs negative for himself

my_sentence: It makes me feel boring or uninterested.
correction: It makes me feel bored or uninterested.
explanation: "-ed" adjectives describe how you feel (bored); "-ing" adjectives describe the thing causing the feeling (boring). You feel bored; the thing is boring.
correct_examples: I was bored during the lecture. | The lecture was boring.
context: Defining positive vs negative for himself

my_sentence: You can talk with them pretty good.
correction: You can talk to them pretty well.
explanation: Use "talk to" for this everyday sense of speaking with someone. "Talk with" is heavier and less common in casual speech.
correct_examples: I need to talk to my boss. | She talked to me about it yesterday.
context: Adjusting your temper with people you dislike

my_sentence: You can talk to them pretty good.
correction: You can talk to them pretty well.
explanation: To modify the verb "talk", use the adverb "well", not the adjective "good". "Good" describes nouns; "well" describes how an action is done.
correct_examples: She speaks English well. | He did well on the test.
context: Adjusting your temper with people you dislike

my_sentence: If you don't do that, you will get criticize and argument.
correction: You will be criticized or argued with.
explanation: For something done to you, use the passive: "be" + past participle ("be criticized"), not "get" + bare verb or a noun. "Argument" is a noun; the passive verb is "argued with".
correct_examples: He was criticized for the mistake. | You will be blamed if it fails.
context: Why you need to be positive at work

my_sentence: It's important to keep positive in our lives.
correction: It's important to be positive in our lives.
explanation: With the adjective "positive" describing a person's general state, use "be positive". "Keep positive" is not the natural collocation the teacher wanted here.
correct_examples: It's important to be positive. | Try to be optimistic about it.
context: Concluding why positivity matters

my_sentence: If you be positive, you will find many opportunities.
correction: If you are a positive person, you will find many opportunities.
explanation: After a subject, use the correct form of "be" ("you are"), not the bare "be". "Positive" is an adjective, so pair it with a noun: "a positive person".
correct_examples: If you are a positive person, life feels lighter. | She is an optimistic person.
context: Why people should be positive

my_sentence: If you are an optimistic, you will find many opportunities.
correction: If you are an optimistic person, you will find many opportunities.
explanation: "Optimistic" is an adjective and cannot stand alone as a noun. It must describe a noun: "an optimistic person".
correct_examples: He is an optimistic person. | She is a hardworking student.
context: Why people should be optimistic

my_sentence: When he or she face the difficulty, he or she can keep calm.
correction: When he or she faces some difficulties, he or she can keep calm.
explanation: "He or she" is third-person singular, so use "faces". Also prefer the plural "some difficulties" for problems in general rather than "the difficulty".
correct_examples: When she faces difficulties, she stays calm. | He faces challenges every day.
context: How an optimistic person handles problems

my_sentence: When they feel disappointed, they can get power from positivity.
correction: When they feel disappointed, they can get strong from being optimistic.
explanation: "Get power" is not idiomatic here; the teacher preferred "get strong". Also use "being optimistic" (a gerund of the verb idea) rather than the noun "positivity" in this slot.
correct_examples: They draw strength from their friends. | She stays strong by being optimistic.
context: How optimism helps when disappointed

my_sentence: A child can stay optimistic if the family give him or her support.
correction: A child can stay optimistic if the family gives him or her support.
explanation: "The family" is treated as a singular subject here, so the verb is "gives", not "give".
correct_examples: If the family gives him support, he thrives. | The team plays well together.
context: How a child becomes optimistic

my_sentence: It can help them take opportunity in order to succeed.
correction: It can help them take some opportunities in order to succeed.
explanation: "Opportunity" is countable; here it needs a plural with a determiner: "some opportunities". Don't leave a singular countable noun bare.
correct_examples: He took several opportunities to learn. | She grabbed every opportunity she could.
context: How positivity helps people succeed

my_sentence: It's important to be positive, so we need to improve our ability, we need to improve our ability.
correction: It's important to be positive, so we need to improve our mental capabilities.
explanation: The teacher repeatedly flagged repeating the same noun/adjective in one sentence. Use a pronoun or a synonym to avoid the repetition, and don't turn the same word back and forth between noun and adjective.
correct_examples: We need to improve our mental capabilities and apply them daily. | She practiced the skill and then taught it to others.
context: Recurring teacher feedback on word repetition

my_sentence: It can make our point clear.
correction: It can make our point clearer.
explanation: Since expressing yourself makes the point *more* clear than before, use the comparative "clearer".
correct_examples: Examples make the idea clearer. | Can you make this sentence clearer?
context: Why expressing yourself well matters

my_sentence: You need to think them first.
correction: You need to think of them first. / Just think of their answers.
explanation: "Think" takes "of" (or "about") before its object: "think of something". Don't drop the preposition.
correct_examples: Think of a number. | I often think about my family.
context: Logical reasoning when sharing ideas

my_sentence: Many of my coworkers can't express them.
correction: Many of my coworkers can't express themselves.
explanation: When the subject and object are the same people, use the reflexive pronoun "themselves", not "them".
correct_examples: They can't express themselves well. | She taught herself to code.
context: People who can't express themselves

my_sentence: They don't know how to listen the question.
correction: They don't know how to listen to the question.
explanation: "Listen" needs "to" before its object: "listen to something/someone".
correct_examples: Listen to me carefully. | I like listening to music.
context: Coworkers who don't focus on the question

my_sentence: Share your idea are not very important in that situation.
correction: Sharing your ideas is not very important in that situation.
explanation: A gerund phrase ("Sharing your ideas") is a single subject and takes a singular verb "is". Use the gerund "Sharing" to start the subject, not the bare verb "Share".
correct_examples: Sharing your ideas is important. | Learning a language takes time.
context: Talking with your boss

my_sentence: Then answer the question rightly.
correction: Then answer the question properly.
explanation: "Rightly" means "justifiably/correctly in judgment" and is rare here. For answering a question well, use "properly" (or "correctly").
correct_examples: Answer the question properly. | Do it properly the first time.
context: Understanding the boss before answering

my_sentence: Some people like to see a lot of sentences and don't give any results.
correction: Some people like to give a lot of ideas but don't give any results.
explanation: You mean people sharing many thoughts, so the right word is "ideas", not "sentences". Also use "but" to contrast the two clauses.
correct_examples: He has a lot of ideas but rarely finishes anything. | She gave many ideas in the meeting.
context: People who beat around the bush

my_sentence: It's a good way to improve our interesting and abilities.
correction: It's a good way to improve our interests and abilities.
explanation: You need the noun "interests" (things you enjoy), not the adjective "interesting".
correct_examples: Reading widens your interests. | The job matches my interests and skills.
context: Choosing your own monthly tasks

my_sentence: You can't become a friend with them and can't cooperate with them.
correction: You cannot become friends with them, and you cannot cooperate with them.
explanation: The fixed expression is "become friends with someone" (plural), because it takes both sides to be friends. Also add the subject "you" before the second "cannot".
correct_examples: We became friends with our neighbors. | She quickly made friends with everyone.
context: Why expressing yourself matters for relationships

my_sentence: Today I was fine.
correction: I had a good day.
explanation: When someone asks "How was your day?" they are asking whether you enjoyed it, so answer with "I had a good/bad day" or "It was stressful." "I was fine" answers "How are you?", not "How was your day?".
correct_examples: I had a good day, thanks. | It was a stressful day.
context: Teacher asking how his day went

my_sentence: And had some meetings with my coworkers.
correction: I had a meeting with my coworkers.
explanation: An English clause needs an explicit subject; don't drop "I". Here it was also one meeting, so use the singular "a meeting".
correct_examples: I had a meeting with my coworkers today. | I attended a meeting this morning.
context: Describing his workday

my_sentence: Customers can use their electronic money to pay in our shopping mall.
correction: ... whenever we buy something at the shopping mall.
explanation: Use "at" for a place where an activity happens (at the shopping mall, at the station). "in the shopping mall" focuses on being physically inside; for shopping/paying, "at" is the natural choice.
correct_examples: I bought it at the shopping mall. | You can pay at any store.
context: Explaining the payment system

my_sentence: It is very important system to the company.
correction: It is a very important system to the company.
explanation: A singular countable noun (system) needs an article. Use "a" before "very important system".
correct_examples: It is a very important system. | This is a key part of the company.
context: Talking about the payment system's importance

my_sentence: We need to collect some informations about our superior.
correction: We need to collect some information about our superior.
explanation: "Information" is uncountable and has no plural form. Never say "informations"; use "some information" or "pieces of information".
correct_examples: I collected some information about them. | He gave me a lot of useful information.
context: How to impress your superiors

my_sentence: Because they will find you can help them a lot.
correction: They will find that you help them a lot.
explanation: After "find" reporting a fact, use the conjunction "that" to introduce the clause, and use the simple present "help" (not "can help") for a general truth.
correct_examples: They will find that you help them a lot. | She realized that he was reliable.
context: Explaining how you impress superiors

my_sentence: We can prepare some documents and solutions to talk with them.
correction: ... to discuss the topics with them.
explanation: "Discuss" takes a direct object (discuss the topics), while "talk" needs a preposition (talk about something). Here "discuss the topics with them" is the natural collocation.
correct_examples: We discussed the plan with the manager. | Let's discuss the details tomorrow.
context: Steps to impress superiors

my_sentence: So when they meet some problems, they will remind us.
correction: If they encounter some of these problems, they will remind us immediately.
explanation: We don't "meet" a problem; the natural collocations are "encounter / run into / come across a problem". "Meet" is for people.
correct_examples: If you encounter any problems, let me know. | We ran into a few issues during testing.
context: Benefits of preparing for superiors

my_sentence: According to the collect information you can get, you can understand the company's next actions.
correction: According to the collected information ...
explanation: Use the past participle "collected" as an adjective before the noun (the collected information = information that has been collected), not the base verb "collect".
correct_examples: Based on the collected data, we made a decision. | The gathered information was very useful.
context: Using information to understand the company

my_sentence: You can understand this thing.
correction: You can understand these things.
explanation: Match the demonstrative to the noun's number. For a plural noun use "these things"; "this thing" is singular.
correct_examples: These things take time. | I can handle these tasks.
context: Understanding company information

my_sentence: It's very useful. You can get a lot.
correction: You can use it a lot, not get it.
explanation: "Get" means to obtain/receive; "use" means to make use of. A system that is useful is one you "use a lot", not "get a lot".
correct_examples: This tool is useful; you can use it a lot. | We use this platform every day.
context: Talking about a system serving millions of users

my_sentence: I want to do something that make me happy.
correction: I want to do something that makes me happy.
explanation: In a relative clause, the verb agrees with the antecedent. "Something" is singular, so use "makes" (something that makes me happy).
correct_examples: I want a job that makes me happy. | She needs something that works for her.
context: What he wants in personal life

my_sentence: It's tired and boring. We need to do something relax.
correction: It's tiring and boring. We need to do something relaxing.
explanation: Use the -ing adjective for what causes a feeling (a tiring/relaxing activity) and the -ed adjective for the person who feels it (I am tired). "Relax" is a verb; the adjective is "relaxing".
correct_examples: The trip was relaxing. | That meeting was tiring, so I felt tired afterwards.
context: Impressing others is tiring; wanting relaxing activities

my_sentence: His behavior is so naive and his speaking was so terrible.
correction: His behavior was so naive and his verbal expressions were so terrible.
explanation: When narrating a past first impression, keep the verbs in the past tense ("was", "were") consistently, not the present "is".
correct_examples: His behavior was very immature. | Back then, she was very shy.
context: Describing a coworker's bad first impression

my_sentence: His speaking was so terrible.
correction: His verbal expressions were so terrible.
explanation: "Speaking" refers to the skill/act of talking in a language; to describe how someone communicates, say "verbal expression" or "the way he speaks/communicates".
correct_examples: His verbal expression was poor. | The way he communicates is unclear.
context: Describing a coworker

my_sentence: We were neighbored in the countryside.
correction: We were neighbours in the countryside.
explanation: "Neighbour" is a noun (the person next door). There is no common verb "to neighbor" here; say "we were neighbours".
correct_examples: We were neighbours for ten years. | They are my neighbours.
context: Describing an old friend from childhood

my_sentence: He regarded everyone as a idiot.
correction: He regarded everyone as an idiot.
explanation: Use "an" before a word starting with a vowel sound (idiot). "A idiot" is incorrect.
correct_examples: She treated him like an idiot. | He is an honest man.
context: Describing an arrogant CTO

my_sentence: Everyone don't like him.
correction: Everyone doesn't like him. / Nobody likes him.
explanation: "Everyone" is grammatically singular, so it takes "doesn't". A more natural way to express this idea is "Nobody likes him."
correct_examples: Everyone here knows the rule. | Nobody likes working overtime.
context: Describing the arrogant CTO

my_sentence: Many of people resigned because of him.
correction: Many of the people resigned because of him. / Many people resigned because of him.
explanation: "Many of" must be followed by a determiner: "many of the people". Without "of", just say "many people".
correct_examples: Many of the employees left. | Many people agreed with the plan.
context: Effect of the arrogant CTO

my_sentence: He was not working there anymore.
correction: He is not working there anymore.
explanation: For a state that is still true now (he no longer works there), use the present "is not working", not the past "was".
correct_examples: He doesn't work here anymore. | She is not living in Beijing anymore.
context: Whether the CTO still works there

my_sentence: They use their beautiful to make money.
correction: They use their beauty to make money.
explanation: "Beautiful" is an adjective; the noun is "beauty". You need a noun as the object of "use".
correct_examples: They use their beauty to sell products. | Her beauty is famous.
context: Talking about the modeling/fashion industry

my_sentence: They don't have enough technology companies.
correction: They don't have enough technological companies.
explanation: Use the adjective "technological" to describe "companies". "Technology companies" exists but the teacher pointed out the adjective form is what's needed to modify the noun here.
correct_examples: It is a technological breakthrough. | The region lacks technological infrastructure.
context: Why he wouldn't move to the Netherlands

my_sentence: Nowadays I deploy application.
correction: Nowadays I deploy an application.
explanation: A singular countable noun (application) needs an article: "deploy an application". For the general activity you could also say "deploy applications".
correct_examples: I deploy an application every week. | We deploy applications to the cloud.
context: Talking about his job and switching to AI

my_sentence: It's a very big, important department.
correction: It's a very big and important department.
explanation: When two adjectives both directly describe the noun, join them with "and" (big and important) rather than only a comma in speech.
correct_examples: It's a big and important project. | She is a kind and patient teacher.
context: Describing the CTO's former department at Sony
